preamble: The transformation of a palatalized voiceless alveolar plosive (`tʲ`) into a voiceless alveolar affricate (`ts`) is a specific type of sound change known as affrication. This process involves the release of the stop consonant immediately followed by a sibilant fricative, effectively combining the two sounds into a single, complex affricate. While palatalization often leads to palato-alveolar or alveolo-palatal affricates, in certain linguistic contexts, the palatalized alveolar stop can develop into an alveolar affricate, often influenced by surrounding vowels or dissimilatory pressures.
phoneticEffects: Affrication, Dissimilation, Depalatalization (in the sense of the palatalized component leading to an alveolar sibilant release rather than a palatal one)

examples.note: 'You became', in the environment preceded by a proclitic with /i/ and followed by /o/ or /a/.
'you came', in the environment preceded by a proclitic with /i/ and followed by /o/ or /a/.
'you made', in the environment preceded by a proclitic with /i/ and followed by /o/ or /a/.
'you sat', in the environment preceded by a proclitic with /i/ and followed by /o/ or /a/.

sources: Brandão, A. (2014). A reference grammar of Paresi-Haliti (Arawak). University of Texas at Austin.
